skill.name: trpc-scaffolder
skill.description: Scaffold type-safe tRPC routers, procedures, and Zod schemas for DevPrep AI style TypeScript projects.
skill.summary: Creates tRPC router and schema boilerplate, then validates registration and type export patterns.
skill.author: ArieGoldkin

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns. Most Markdown, template, and example hits are false positives from code fences, tRPC method names, relative documentation links, and words such as mutation. The real concern is unvalidated user-supplied router and schema names in shell scripts that can affect output paths and generated code.

medium_findings.title: Unvalidated Scaffold Names Can Influence Output Paths
medium_findings.description: The router and schema generator scripts derive output filenames directly from positional arguments. A name containing traversal characters can escape the intended router or schema filename boundary if parent directories exist, causing unintended file creation or overwrite within the project workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts use user-supplied names in OUTPUT_FILE paths without allowlist validation. The behavior is legitimate scaffolding, but the missing validation creates a real path integrity risk.

medium_findings.title: User Input Is Substituted Into Generated TypeScript Without Escaping
medium_findings.description: The scripts pass router, entity, and procedure names into sed replacement expressions and generated identifiers. Special replacement characters or invalid TypeScript identifier characters can corrupt generated files or produce unsafe boilerplate that requires review before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sed substitutions are directly parameterized by CLI arguments. Shell quoting limits command injection, but replacement escaping and identifier validation are absent.

low_findings.title: Markdown and Example Code Trigger Static False Positives
low_findings.description: Most external command, weak cryptography, and hidden file alerts occur in documentation, examples, or templates. They describe tRPC usage, shell commands in fenced blocks, relative docs links, and method names such as mutation rather than malicious behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown examples, comments, imports, or local documentation links. No evidence of weak cryptography, malicious hidden file access, or prompt injection was found in these contexts.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Scripts Perform Filesystem Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes Bash scripts that create router and schema files in frontend tRPC directories. This capability is expected for the skill, but publication should warn users to review generated paths and run scripts only in trusted repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Filesystem writes are explicit and central to the skill. The behavior is not malicious, but it increases risk because inputs are not constrained to safe identifiers.

dangerous_patterns.title: Repository Validation Uses find and grep
dangerous_patterns.description: The validation script scans local router and schema files with find and grep. This is normal local validation and does not show network access or data exfiltration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands read local project files to verify tRPC registration and type exports. I found no evidence of network transfer, secret access, or destructive operations.

content.value_statement: Creating tRPC routers and Zod schemas by hand can create naming drift and missing validation. This skill provides focused scaffolding, examples, and checks for consistent TypeScript API work.
actual_capabilities: Creates new tRPC router files from a local template.
Creates Zod schema files with inferred TypeScript type exports.
Generates procedure snippets for query and mutation handlers.
Checks router registration in the app router file.
Checks schema files for exported inferred types.
Provides examples and documentation for common tRPC patterns.
limitations: Scripts assume a DevPrep AI style frontend directory layout.
Router and schema names are not validated before file generation.
Generated code includes TODO placeholders that require manual implementation.
The validation script uses grep patterns and can miss complex registration styles.

use_cases.title: Add a Feature Endpoint
use_cases.description: Create a router, schema, and procedure outline for a new product feature.

use_cases.title: Standardize API Patterns
use_cases.description: Use shared templates to keep tRPC naming, Zod validation, and type exports consistent.

use_cases.title: Review tRPC Setup
use_cases.description: Run local checks for router registration and schema type exports before code review.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Router
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new domain router.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the tRPC scaffolder to create a router for the user domain. Show me the generated file path and registration step.

prompt_templates.title: Create Matching Schemas
prompt_templates.scenario: Add validation for a new API feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create Zod schemas for a hint endpoint with input and output types. Include the inferred TypeScript type exports.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Procedure Snippet
prompt_templates.scenario: Extend an existing router with a typed procedure.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a query procedure snippet named getHints for the ai router. Use input and output schemas and explain where to paste it.

prompt_templates.title: Validate and Diagnose Setup
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced check before review or merge.
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate the current tRPC setup, identify missing registrations or type exports, and recommend exact fixes for each issue.

output_examples.input: Create a router for the user domain.
output_examples.output: A new user router file is created in the frontend tRPC router directory, with next steps for schema creation and app router registration.

output_examples.input: Add a getHints query to the ai router.
output_examples.output: A procedure outline is produced with input validation, output validation, a query handler, and TODO markers for business logic.

output: Reports whether the app router file exists.
Lists router files found in the project.
Flags missing imports, registrations, or schema type exports.

best_practices: Use simple alphanumeric router, schema, and procedure names before running scripts.
Review every generated file before committing it to the repository.
Run type checks after scaffolding to catch schema and router drift.
anti_patterns: Do not pass paths, slashes, or shell metacharacters as scaffold names.
Do not publish generated TODO handlers without implementing business logic.
Do not assume grep-based validation replaces TypeScript compilation or tests.

faq.question: Does this skill generate complete business logic?
faq.answer: No. It creates tRPC and Zod structure with TODO markers for the developer to complete.

faq.question: Which projects does it fit best?
faq.answer: It fits TypeScript projects that use tRPC, Zod, and the expected DevPrep AI frontend folder layout.

faq.question: Can it add procedures to existing routers?
faq.answer: Yes. It outputs a query or mutation snippet for manual insertion into an existing router.

faq.question: Does it modify the app router automatically?
faq.answer: No. It prints registration guidance, but the developer must update the app router file.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but its scripts write files and do not validate scaffold names before building paths.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
